# Requirements

The requirements are derived from industrial specification documents provided by AES. The specification categorizes features into Bronze (baseline), Silver (optional), and Gold (advanced) levels. In this thesis, the Bronze-level requirements are adopted as the mandatory design baseline. Silver- and Gold-level requirements are not implemented and are considered only as potential extensions for future work.

The following requirements define the complete functional and performance scope addressed in this work.

## (Bronze-Level) Requirements

- Support for at least four LED channels (e.g., RGBW)
- 16-bit dimming resolution per channel
- Brightness control via low-side PWM
- PWM frequency > 400 Hz to prevent visible flicker
- Independent configuration of each channel
- Configuration data shall be stored internally until changed
- Daisy-chain capability with identical input and output interfaces
- Update latency such that the 25th IC in the chain updates within < 25 ms
- Configuration via synchronous serial data from an external controller
- Data interface voltage range of 3 V to 5 V
- Operation from an 18 V to 36 V supply, without directly supplying LED power
- Emphasis on low power digital operation


# Preferred Approach

This thesis follows a structured engineering workflow to ensure the digital design is correct, reliable, and suitable for later integration into a mixed-signal IC.

The work begins with understanding the system from both an architectural and functional perspective. The requirements from AES and the behaviour of the LED1642GW are studied to define what the digital subsystem must achieve. Once the requirements are clear, a behavioural model is created in Matlab/Simulink. This model allows early functional validation and provides a reference for later hardware implementation.

After validating the model, the digital micro-architecture is designed in SystemVerilog. The RTL is structured into clean, modular blocks that reflect the behaviour captured in the model. Verification is carried out using cocotb, which allows flexible Python-based testing. The design is finally implemented on a GateMate FPGA to observe real hardware behaviour, including timing, PWM output, and state-machine operation.

Throughout all steps, documentation is maintained to ensure traceability from requirements through implementation to validation. The thesis documentation will be prepared using a markup-based workflow (LaTeX or Markdown), enabling efficient version control with Git and seamless integration with the development environment used for SystemVerilog, MATLAB/Simulink, and Python code, while remaining compatible with standard academic templates.
